approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all pairs (i, j) in the array, compute lcm(i, j) using the formula lcm(a, b) = a * b / gcd(a, b), and sum the results. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force LCM Sum
ladders.desc: Compute sum of LCM for all pairs in an array using O(n²) brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the LCM sum problem using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: we need to consider every possible pair (i, j) in the array, compute their LCM, and accumulate the sum. For each pair, we use the mathematical relationship lcm(a, b) = a * b / gcd(a, b), where gcd is the greatest common divisor. This works because LCM represents the smallest positive integer divisible by both numbers, and the formula derives from the fundamental theorem of arithmetic. The algorithm iterates through all pairs with nested loops, computes gcd using Euclid's algorithm (or built-in function), calculates LCM via the formula, and adds to a running total. Time complexity is O(n² * log(min(a,b))) because we have O(n²) pairs and each gcd computation takes O(log(min(a,b))) time. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large n but serves as a baseline solution. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete solution by calling the gcd function (from L1) repeatedly. Common mistakes include integer overflow when multiplying a*b before division (mitigate by using long integers or dividing first), forgetting to handle pairs where i=j (LCM of a number with itself is the number itself), and incorrect array indexing. Real code pattern: initialize sum=0; for i from 0 to n-1: for j from i to n-1: g=gcd(arr[i], arr[j]); lcm=arr[i]*arr[j]//g; sum+=lcm; return sum.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — GCD Computation
ladders.desc: Implement the greatest common divisor function using Euclid's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on computing the greatest common divisor (GCD), which is essential for calculating LCM via the formula lcm(a,b)=a*b/gcd(a,b). The intuition behind Euclid's algorithm is based on the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This works because any common divisor of a and b must also divide their difference, and by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder, we reduce the problem size until we reach gcd(x,0)=x. The algorithm takes two integers a and b, and while b is not 0: set temp = b, b = a mod b, a = temp. Return a. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) as each step reduces the numbers significantly. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for every pair in the brute force solution. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops if not updating variables correctly, and integer overflow in languages with fixed-size integers. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return abs(a).

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=12, b=18, b!=0 so compute: temp=18, b=12%18=12, a=18
Step 2: a=18, b=12, b!=0 so compute: temp=12, b=18%12=6, a=12
Step 3: a=12, b=6, b!=0 so compute: temp=6, b=12%6=0, a=6
Step 4: b=0, return a=6

Step 1: a=7, b=13, b!=0 so compute: temp=13, b=7%13=7, a=13
Step 2: a=13, b=7, b!=0 so compute: temp=7, b=13%7=6, a=7
Step 3: a=7, b=6, b!=0 so compute: temp=6, b=7%6=1, a=6
Step 4: a=6, b=1, b!=0 so compute: temp=1, b=6%1=0, a=1
Step 5: b=0, return a=1

ladders.title: Core Logic — LCM via GCD Formula
ladders.desc: Calculate LCM of two numbers using the formula lcm(a,b) = a*b / gcd(a,b).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core mathematical insight: lcm(a,b) = a*b / gcd(a,b). The intuition is that the product a*b contains all prime factors of both numbers, but the gcd represents the overlapping factors counted twice. Dividing by gcd removes this duplication, leaving the smallest common multiple. This works because of the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer has a unique prime factorization. The algorithm: compute gcd of a and b (using Euclid's algorithm from L1), then compute lcm = (a*b) // gcd. Use integer division to avoid floating-point issues. Time complexity is O(log(min(a,b))) dominated by gcd computation. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This calculation is performed for each pair in the brute force solution. Common mistakes include using floating-point division (which can cause precision errors), not handling large numbers that might overflow when multiplying (use long integers or divide first: a//gcd * b), and forgetting to ensure inputs are positive. Real code pattern: def lcm(a,b): g=gcd(a,b); return a//g * b.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute gcd(4,6): gcd=2
Step 2: Compute lcm = 4*6 / 2 = 24/2 = 12

Step 1: Compute gcd(15,20): gcd=5
Step 2: Compute lcm = 15*20 / 5 = 300/5 = 60

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs (i,j) from an array where i ≤ j.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on generating all pairs of indices (i,j) from an array, which is necessary for the brute force approach. The intuition is that to compute the sum of LCM for all pairs, we need to consider each pair exactly once. We iterate with nested loops where the outer loop goes from 0 to n-1 and the inner loop goes from i to n-1 (to avoid duplicates and include pairs where i=j). This ensures we cover all combinations without repetition. The algorithm: initialize an empty list of pairs; for i from 0 to n-1: for j from i to n-1: add pair (arr[i], arr[j]) to list. Time complexity is O(n²) as we generate n*(n+1)/2 pairs. Space complexity is O(n²) if storing all pairs, but can be O(1) if processing on the fly. Connection to L0: This pair generation is the outer structure of the brute force solution. Common mistakes include double-counting pairs by using j from 0 to n-1 (which counts (i,j) and (j,i) separately), incorrect array bounds, and not including pairs where i=j (which are valid for LCM sum). Real code pattern: pairs=[]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): pairs.append((arr[i], arr[j])).

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0,1,2
i=0: j from 0 to 2: pairs (arr[0],arr[0])=(1,1), (1,2), (1,3)
i=1: j from 1 to 2: pairs (arr[1],arr[1])=(2,2), (2,3)
i=2: j from 2 to 2: pair (arr[2],arr[2])=(3,3)
Total pairs: 6

Array indices: 0,1
i=0: j from 0 to 1: pairs (4,4), (4,5)
i=1: j from 1 to 1: pair (5,5)
Total pairs: 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Perform modulo and integer division operations correctly.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental arithmetic operations of modulo (%) and integer division (//), which are crucial for GCD and LCM computations. Modulo (a % b) gives the remainder when a is divided by b. Integer division (a // b) gives the quotient without remainder. The intuition: modulo is used in Euclid's algorithm to reduce numbers, and integer division is used in LCM formula to avoid floating-point errors. These operations work because they are based on the division algorithm: for integers a and b (b≠0), there exist unique q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = b*q + r with 0 ≤ r < |b|. Here, q = a // b and r = a % b. The algorithm is straightforward: use % for remainder and // for integer division. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L1 and L2: modulo is used in gcd computation, and integer division in lcm calculation. Common mistakes include dividing by zero, confusion between / (float division) and // (integer division) in Python, and incorrect handling of negative numbers (modulo behavior varies by language; in Python, a % b has same sign as b). Real code pattern: remainder = a % b; quotient = a // b.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding LCM and GCD
ladders.desc: Define and compute LCM and GCD for two numbers using basic examples.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the foundational concepts of Least Common Multiple (LCM) and Greatest Common Divisor (GCD). LCM of two numbers is the smallest positive integer divisible by both numbers. GCD is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers. Intuition: think of LCM as finding a common meeting point in multiples, and GCD as finding shared factors. For example, for numbers 4 and 6: multiples of 4 are 4,8,12,16,...; multiples of 6 are 6,12,18,...; LCM is 12. Divisors of 4 are 1,2,4; divisors of 6 are 1,2,3,6; GCD is 2. These concepts work because of the properties of integers and divisibility. The algorithm for beginners: to find GCD, list all divisors and pick the largest; to find LCM, list multiples and pick the smallest common one. Time complexity is O(min(a,b)) for naive methods. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: these definitions lead to the formula lcm(a,b)=a*b/gcd(a,b). Common mistakes include confusing LCM with GCD, not considering 1 as a divisor, and forgetting that LCM is at least as large as the numbers. Real code pattern for naive: gcd=1; for i from 1 to min(a,b): if a%i==0 and b%i==0: gcd=i; lcm=max(a,b); while lcm%a!=0 or lcm%b!=0: lcm+=1.

examples.trace:
GCD: divisors of 4: 1,2,4; divisors of 6: 1,2,3,6; common divisors: 1,2; largest is 2.
LCM: multiples of 4: 4,8,12,16,...; multiples of 6: 6,12,18,...; common multiples: 12,...; smallest is 12.

GCD: divisors of 5: 1,5; divisors of 7: 1,7; common divisor: 1; largest is 1.
LCM: multiples of 5: 5,10,15,20,25,30,35,...; multiples of 7: 7,14,21,28,35,...; common multiple: 35; smallest is 35.
